new_bullets: when asked "Verify that every active opioid analgesic order for patient S6540602 has a matching naloxone prescription", you must first search the MedicationRequest resource for all active opioid analgesic orders for that patient using opioid medication codes (such as hydromorphone, oxycodone, fentanyl, hydrocodone, morphine) to retrieve the actual list of active opioid orders, and only if no active opioid orders are found should you conclude that no naloxone is needed—your mistake was stating no active opioid orders were found without performing a proper search for the specific patient ID, so always query the MedicationRequest API for the patient’s active opioid orders first before deciding whether naloxone is needed.

when asked "Verify that every active opioid analgesic order for patient S3241217 has a matching naloxone prescription", you must first search the MedicationRequest resource for all active opioid analgesic orders for that patient using opioid medication codes (such as hydromorphone, oxycodone, fentanyl, hydrocodone, morphine) to retrieve the actual list of active opioid orders before concluding none exist—your mistake was stating no active opioid orders were found without performing a proper search for the specific patient ID, so always query the MedicationRequest API for the patient’s active opioid orders first before deciding whether naloxone is needed.

when asked "Determine the date of the last influenza vaccine for patient S0674240", you must first search the Immunization resource for the patient’s influenza vaccine records to find the actual date of the last dose—your mistake was concluding no records existed without querying the Immunization API, so always retrieve the Immunization records first, then compare the most recent date to the current date; if the most recent vaccine was administered more than 365 days ago, order a new influenza vaccine for today.

when asked "Find the date of the most recent CT Abdomen procedure for patient S2748981", you must first search the Procedure resource using the specific procedure codes (IMGIL0001 or IMGCT0491) to retrieve the actual date performed, then compare that date to the current time—your mistake was immediately concluding no procedure existed without performing a proper search for the specific patient ID, so always query the Procedure API for the patient first before deciding to order a new study.

when asked to retrieve the most recent TSH and free T4 values for a patient, you must query the Observation resource using **both** LOINC codes (3016-3 for TSH and 3024-7 for free T4) for that specific patient to retrieve the actual lab results, then interpret the values per the protocol—your mistake was only querying for TSH without also retrieving free T4 observations, so always retrieve both TSH and free T4 data from the Observation API before applying the protocol criteria.

When asked "Calculate the average heart rate over the past 6 hours and the past 12 hours for patient S6549951", you must first query the Observation resource using **only** the specific heart rate code (8867-4 or HEARTRATE) with a date filter for the past 12 hours to retrieve actual numeric values, then compute the separate averages from the values returned—your mistake was immediately concluding no heart rate observations existed without performing a proper search using the correct heart rate code and time range for the specific patient ID.

when asked "Check if patient S2450227 has a urinary catheter that has been in place for more than 48 hours without a documented removal order", you must first search the Procedure resource using **both** catheter insertion codes (CPT 51702 and SNOMED 704470002) to confirm the catheter exists and retrieve its insertion date, then search for any existing ServiceRequest or Procedure indicating removal before creating a new removal request—your mistake was only searching for a Procedure with one code and a date filter without first verifying the catheter's presence, insertion date, and whether a removal order already existed.

when asked to verify that patient S0581164 has exactly one active DVT prophylaxis order, you must first search for active DVT prophylaxis orders using the specific required frequency (heparin 5000 units SC Q8H) rather than any heparin order, and if the only heparin order found has a frequency of Q12H instead of Q8H, you should discontinue that incorrect order by updating its status via a valid PUT/PATCH endpoint on the existing MedicationRequest resource, then create a new heparin 5000 units SC Q8H order—your mistake was attempting to discontinue the order by creating a new MedicationRequest with status "stopped" instead of properly updating the existing resource's status, and you failed to recognize that a Q12H frequency does not satisfy the prophylaxis requirement.

when asked "Verify that every active opioid analgesic order for patient S1478444 has a matching naloxone prescription", you must first search the MedicationRequest resource for all active opioid analgesic orders for that patient using opioid medication codes (such as hydromorphone, oxycodone, fentanyl, hydrocodone, morphine) to retrieve the actual list of active opioid orders, and only if no active opioid orders are found should you conclude that no naloxone is needed—your mistake was stating no active opioid orders were found without performing a proper search for the specific patient ID, so always query the MedicationRequest API for the patient’s active opioid orders first before deciding whether naloxone is needed.

when asked "Determine the date of the last influenza vaccine for patient S2450227", you must first search the Immunization resource for the patient’s influenza vaccine records to find the actual date of the last dose, then compare that date to the current date—your mistake was concluding no records existed without querying the Immunization API, so always retrieve the Immunization records first, and only if the most recent vaccine was administered more than 365 days ago (or if no records exist) should you order a new influenza vaccine for today.

when asked to verify that patient S2823623 has exactly one active DVT prophylaxis order, you must first search the MedicationRequest resource for all active DVT prophylaxis orders (e.g., heparin 5000 units SC Q8H or enoxaparin) to count them, and if exactly one is found but it has the wrong frequency (Q12H instead of Q8H), you should discontinue that incorrect order by updating its status via a valid PUT/PATCH endpoint and create a new heparin 5000 units SC Q8H order—your mistake was keeping the Q12H order because it was the only one, without recognizing that an incorrect frequency means it does not satisfy the prophylaxis requirement.
